nativeRange.habitat: Bayer's Moray inhabits exposed benches and seaward reef faces, typically in the upper 20 metres of the water column, though it has been recorded at depths of up to 64 m. In the wild it tends to behave secretively, sheltering inside reef crevices and caves, with some activity reported at night.

decorPreferences: dense live rock with caves and crevices sized to conceal the full body
tight-fitting, weighted lid with no gaps

diet.notes: Primarily a fish predator in the wild. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen meaty foods such as silversides, krill, whole shrimp, squid, and chopped fish. Newly acquired individuals may initially require live prey (ghost shrimp, small feeder fish) before transitioning to prepared or frozen foods. Feeding with long tongs or a feeding stick is generally advised; the species has poor eyesight and has been known to bite by accident. Adults are typically fed two to three times per week.

goodWith: large, robust fish not small enough to be swallowed
avoidWith: all crustaceans — shrimp, crabs, hermit crabs, lobsters (natural prey)
other moray eels unless tank is very large and both introduced simultaneously

summary: Bayer's Moray Eel (Enchelycore bayeri) is an uncommonly encountered Indo-Pacific reef eel reaching up to 70 cm. Found from the Red Sea to French Polynesia on exposed seaward reef faces, it is seldom available in the aquarium trade and requires a large, tightly covered tank with ample rocky hiding places.
careNotes:
Bayer's Moray Eel is considered a relatively uncommon species in the aquarium trade and is seldom encountered compared to more widely kept morays. Its care requirements are broadly consistent with other medium-to-large members of the family Muraenidae (the moray eels).

A minimum tank of around 400 litres is generally advised for an adult, with a footprint of at least 150 cm in length to allow the eel adequate territory and room to move. Dense live rock arranged to provide caves and crevices large enough to conceal the full body is considered important — morays spend much of the day sheltering inside reef structures rather than swimming in the open. Rockwork should be stable and firmly secured, as a large eel can dislodge unsupported pieces.

A tightly sealed, weighted aquarium lid with no gaps is considered essential. Moray eels are widely documented as capable escape artists that can push through loose-fitting covers or squeeze through surprisingly small openings, and an escaped eel is unlikely to survive outside the tank. An efficient protein skimmer and robust biological filtration are generally advised to handle the heavy bioload a carnivore of this size produces.

Bayer's Moray is generally not considered reef-safe. It will prey on any crustacean or small fish sharing the aquarium, including shrimp, crabs, hermit crabs, and any fish small enough to swallow. Tankmates are best limited to large, robust fish that are clearly too big to be eaten.

In the wild, this eel tends to be nocturnal or crepuscular (most active at dusk and dawn), remaining hidden in reef crevices during daylight hours. In the first weeks after introduction, feeding is best attempted after lights go out, when the eel is more likely to be active and receptive.
breedingNotes: As with most moray eels, captive reproduction is not considered achievable under typical home aquarium conditions.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: LiveAquaria does not have a dedicated species page for Enchelycore bayeri. Care parameters were extrapolated from LiveAquaria profiles for comparable Muraenidae species: Jeweled Moray Eel (Muraena lentiginosa, 61 cm, 300 L minimum) and Yellow-Head Moray Eel (Gymnothorax fimbriatus, 76–93 cm, 500 L minimum). Water parameters (SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, temp 72–78°F) are consistent across all LiveAquaria moray profiles.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Primary source for taxonomy, adult size (max 70 cm TL, common 61 cm), native range (Indo-Pacific: Indian Ocean incl. Red Sea to French Polynesia; absent Hawaiian Islands), preferred temperature range (24.9–28.9°C), and habitat description (seaward reef benches; secretive; sometimes nocturnal). No salinity or pH data provided. IUCN status: Least Concern.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding has been documented for this species.
